bytes.topic: MLOps Philosophy — why reproducibility, versioning, and automation matter in ML
bytes.title: ONNX MLOps — Reproducibility, Versioning, and Automation Built Into the ModelProto
bytes.body:
ONNX's `ModelProto` is your reproducibility contract. The `opset_import` field pins operator semantics — a silent opset bump during re-export can silently alter numerics or fail on a target EP. Lock it explicitly on every export and treat changes as breaking versions.

For versioning, ONNX gives you `metadata_props` — a key-value store embedded directly in the `.onnx` file. Stamp your git SHA, dataset hash, and training run ID there. The artifact becomes self-describing and survives S3 bucket migrations or model registry refactors without losing provenance.

Automation gates belong at two points: `onnx.checker.check_model()` catches structural violations, but an `onnxruntime.InferenceSession` dry-run with shape assertions catches EP-specific failures (TensorRT, CUDA) that the checker misses entirely. Run both in CI on every PR that touches model export code.

Reproducible inference also means locking `SessionOptions`. Two sessions on the same `.onnx` file with different `GraphOptimizationLevel` values produce different latency profiles and can diverge numerically near float boundaries — especially with fused ops enabled.
bytes.code_snippet:
import onnx
import onnxruntime as ort

model = onnx.load("model.onnx")

# Stamp provenance directly into the artifact
for key, value in {"git_sha": "a3f9c12", "dataset_hash": "sha256:d4e5f6"}.items():
    entry = model.metadata_props.add()
    entry.key, entry.value = key, value

onnx.checker.check_model(model)
onnx.save(model, "model_versioned.onnx")

# Lock session options — same file, same behavior across machines
opts = ort.SessionOptions()
opts.graph_optimization_level = ort.GraphOptimizationLevel.ORT_ENABLE_EXTENDED
opts.inter_op_num_threads = 1
session = ort.InferenceSession("model_versioned.onnx", sess_options=opts)

bytes.topic: Experiment Tracking — logging metrics, params, artifacts, comparing runs
bytes.title: ONNX Experiment Tracking — Embed Metrics and Params Directly in the Model Proto
bytes.body:
ONNX model protos carry a `metadata_props` field — a repeated list of `StringStringEntryProto` — that lets you stamp run ID, hyperparameters, and eval metrics directly into the `.onnx` binary. No external tracker required; the artifact is self-describing.

After training, call `onnx.load()`, iterate your params dict, and for each entry call `model.metadata_props.add()`, setting `.key` and `.value`. Then `onnx.save()` commits it. The metadata travels with every copy of the file.

For inference-time metrics, enable ONNX Runtime's built-in profiler via `SessionOptions.enable_profiling = True` and `SessionOptions.profile_file_prefix`. Calling `sess.end_profiling()` flushes a Chrome-trace JSON containing per-op latency in microseconds. Parse it to compute P99 inference time and write that back into `metadata_props` before archiving the artifact.

To compare runs, load each `.onnx` and build `{p.key: p.value for p in model.metadata_props}`. Sort by `val_acc` or `p99_ms` across files. Your full experiment history lives inside the artifacts — queryable with plain Python, no tracking server, no schema migrations, no UI to maintain.
bytes.code_snippet:
import onnx, onnxruntime as ort

# Embed hyperparams + eval metrics into the ONNX proto
model = onnx.load("model.onnx")
for key, val in {"run_id": "exp_042", "lr": "1e-3", "val_acc": "0.923"}.items():
    e = model.metadata_props.add()
    e.key, e.value = key, str(val)
onnx.save(model, "model_exp042.onnx")

# Capture per-op latency via ORT's built-in profiler
opts = ort.SessionOptions()
opts.enable_profiling = True
opts.profile_file_prefix = "run_exp042"
sess = ort.InferenceSession("model_exp042.onnx", opts)
sess.end_profiling()  # writes run_exp042_<ts>.json (Chrome trace, per-op μs)

# Compare runs — read metadata back from any .onnx file
props = {p.key: p.value for p in onnx.load("model_exp042.onnx").metadata_props}
print(props)  # {'run_id': 'exp_042', 'lr': '1e-3', 'val_acc': '0.923'}

bytes.topic: MLflow — tracking server, model registry, projects, model serving
bytes.title: ONNX + MLflow — Tracking, Registry, and Serving Your InferenceSession
bytes.body: MLflow has a first-class ONNX flavor via `mlflow.onnx`, which wraps your `onnxruntime.InferenceSession` through the full MLOps lifecycle. When logging, pass the raw `onnx.ModelProto` object — MLflow serializes it internally and records the opset version and IR version as run metadata automatically. Use `mlflow.onnx.log_model(onnx_model, artifact_path="model")` inside a run, then register it with `MlflowClient.create_model_version()` pointing to the run artifact URI. At serving time, `mlflow models serve -m models:/MyOnnxModel/Production -p 5001` spins up a REST endpoint that deserializes the proto, creates an `InferenceSession` with the default `CPUExecutionProvider`, and accepts JSON payloads with an `inputs` key mapping to named input tensors. For custom execution providers (CUDA, TensorRT), skip the built-in server — load via `mlflow.onnx.load_model()`, which returns the `ModelProto`, then wrap it yourself: `ort.InferenceSession(model.SerializeToString(), providers=["CUDAExecutionProvider"])`. Combine this with MLflow Projects to pin the `onnxruntime` version and opset in `MLproject` for reproducible training pipelines.
bytes.code_snippet:
import mlflow
import mlflow.onnx
import onnxruntime as ort

with mlflow.start_run():
    mlflow.onnx.log_model(onnx_model_proto, artifact_path="model")
    run_id = mlflow.active_run().info.run_id

client = mlflow.MlflowClient()
model_uri = f"runs:/{run_id}/model"
client.create_registered_model("MyOnnxModel")
client.create_model_version(name="MyOnnxModel", source=model_uri, run_id=run_id)

# Load and wrap with custom EP
proto = mlflow.onnx.load_model("models:/MyOnnxModel/Production")
sess = ort.InferenceSession(
    proto.SerializeToString(),
    providers=["CUDAExecutionProvider", "CPUExecutionProvider"]
)

bytes.topic: Weights & Biases — experiment tracking, sweeps for hyperparameter search, reports
bytes.title: ONNX Runtime Tuning with W&B — Sweep SessionOptions, Not Guesswork
bytes.body:
ONNX Runtime performance is highly sensitive to SessionOptions — graph_optimization_level, intra_op_num_threads, execution_mode, and execution provider ordering all interact non-linearly. Manually tuning these is guesswork. W&B Sweeps with Bayesian optimization turns it into a proper search.

Define a sweep config targeting GraphOptimizationLevel values (ORT_ENABLE_BASIC=1, ORT_ENABLE_EXTENDED=2, ORT_ENABLE_ALL=99) and thread counts, then let wandb.agent drive ort.InferenceSession construction across trials. Log latency_ms or throughput as the metric. After 20–30 runs, W&B's parallel coordinates plot in Reports reveals which SessionOptions combos dominate — often ORT_ENABLE_ALL with 4 intra_op_num_threads outperforms the naive all-cores default due to NUMA contention.

For multi-EP models, extend the sweep to include providers ordering (["CUDAExecutionProvider", "CPUExecutionProvider"] vs ["TensorrtExecutionProvider", ...]) and enable per-node profiling via sess_options.enable_profiling=True to log node-level hotspots as W&B artifacts. W&B Reports let you diff these runs side-by-side — shareable links make EP trade-off decisions reviewable by the whole team without re-running locally.
bytes.code_snippet:
import wandb, onnxruntime as ort, numpy as np, time

sweep_config = {
    "method": "bayes",
    "metric": {"name": "latency_ms", "goal": "minimize"},
    "parameters": {
        "graph_opt_level": {"values": [1, 2, 99]},
        "intra_op_threads": {"min": 1, "max": 8},
    },
}

def run():
    wandb.init()
    opts = ort.SessionOptions()
    opts.graph_optimization_level = ort.GraphOptimizationLevel(wandb.config.graph_opt_level)
    opts.intra_op_num_threads = wandb.config.intra_op_threads
    sess = ort.InferenceSession("model.onnx", sess_options=opts, providers=["CPUExecutionProvider"])
    x = np.random.randn(1, 3, 224, 224).astype(np.float32)
    [sess.run(None, {"input": x}) for _ in range(10)]  # warmup
    t = time.perf_counter()
    [sess.run(None, {"input": x}) for _ in range(100)]
    wandb.log({"latency_ms": (time.perf_counter() - t) * 10})

sweet_id = wandb.sweep(sweep_config, project="onnx-rt-tuning")
wandb.agent(sweet_id, run, count=20)

bytes.topic: DVC — data versioning, pipeline stages, remote storage, reproducible pipelines
bytes.title: ONNX Model Versioning with DVC — Reproducible ML Pipelines for ONNX Exports
bytes.body:
When you export a PyTorch or TensorFlow model to ONNX format, the resulting `.onnx` file is a binary artifact that changes with every retrain. Without versioning, you lose traceability between your training data, preprocessing logic, and the specific opset-level graph that went to production. DVC solves this by treating your `.onnx` files as first-class versioned artifacts, stored remotely (S3, Azure Blob, GCS) while only a lightweight `.dvc` pointer file lives in Git.

Define a DVC pipeline with stages: `preprocess → train → export_onnx → validate_onnx`. The `export_onnx` stage runs `torch.onnx.export()` with a pinned `opset_version` and outputs `model.onnx` as a tracked artifact. The `validate_onnx` stage runs `onnxruntime.InferenceSession` against a golden input/output fixture to catch shape or dtype regressions before the model reaches your serving layer. With `dvc repro`, the entire pipeline reruns only when upstream deps (data hash, training script, opset config) change — giving you byte-for-byte reproducible ONNX graphs tied to a Git commit.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dvc.yaml
stages:
  export_onnx:
    cmd: python export.py --opset 17 --output model.onnx
    deps:
      - train/model.pt
      - export.py
    params:
      - params.yaml:
          - opset_version
    outs:
      - model.onnx

  validate_onnx:
    cmd: python validate.py --model model.onnx --fixture tests/golden.npz
    deps:
      - model.onnx
      - validate.py
      - tests/golden.npz

bytes.topic: Model Registry — versioning models, staging vs production, approval workflows
bytes.title: ONNX Model Registry — Staging, Production, and Opset-Aware Approval Gates with MLflow
bytes.body:
ONNX models stored in MLflow's registry expose three mutable stages per version: `None`, `Staging`, and `Production`. Each `mlflow.onnx.log_model()` call creates a new `ModelVersion` that bundles the full `onnx.ModelProto` — including `opset_import` metadata — critical because an opset-17 model silently falls back to CPU on ORT execution providers that only support opset-15.

The approval workflow maps to `MlflowClient.transition_model_version_stage()`. Typical gate: CI logs the ONNX artifact, spins an `onnxruntime.InferenceSession` against the Staging URI (`models:/name/Staging`), validates the opset, then promotes to `Production` with `archive_existing_versions=True` — atomically retiring the prior champion version.

For canary rollouts, pin ORT sessions to an explicit version number (`models:/resnet50-v2/3`) rather than the `Production` alias. The alias can flip mid-request during a promotion; a pinned URI references an immutable artifact. Always gate on `proto.opset_import[0].version` before promotion — a mismatched opset nukes GPU throughput with no error, just silent CPU fallback on DirectML and CUDA EPs.
bytes.code_snippet:
import mlflow, mlflow.onnx, onnxruntime as ort
from mlflow.tracking import MlflowClient

with mlflow.start_run():
    mlflow.onnx.log_model(model, "model", registered_model_name="resnet50-v2")

client = MlflowClient()
client.transition_model_version_stage("resnet50-v2", version=3, stage="Staging")

# Approval gate: validate opset before production promotion
proto = mlflow.onnx.load_model("models:/resnet50-v2/Staging")
assert proto.opset_import[0].version >= 17, "ORT EP requires opset >= 17"

client.transition_model_version_stage(
    "resnet50-v2", version=3, stage="Production", archive_existing_versions=True
)
session = ort.InferenceSession(proto.SerializeToString())

bytes.topic: ONNX — cross-framework model format, export process, runtime inference
bytes.title: ONNX Runtime — Export PyTorch Models and Run Inference Across Frameworks
bytes.body: ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) gives you a single serialized graph that any compliant runtime can execute — PyTorch, TensorFlow, and scikit-learn all export to it. The export process freezes your model into a static computational graph: `torch.onnx.export()` traces through a forward pass using dummy inputs, capturing every op as an ONNX node. Dynamic axes let you preserve variable batch sizes or sequence lengths without re-exporting. Once you have the `.onnx` file, `onnxruntime.InferenceSession` loads it — no Python ML framework required at inference time. ORT selects an execution provider automatically (CPUExecutionProvider, CUDAExecutionProvider, TensorrtExecutionProvider) based on availability. Input names must exactly match those registered during export — mismatches throw at session creation, not at `run()`. Use `onnx.checker.check_model()` before deployment to validate graph integrity and opset compatibility. Opset version matters: exporting at opset 17 may produce ops unsupported by older ORT builds. Pin both together in CI. For quantization, `onnxruntime.quantization.quantize_dynamic()` converts float32 weights to int8 post-export with no retraining.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
import onnxruntime as ort
import numpy as np

# Export
model = MyModel().eval()
dummy = torch.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
torch.onnx.export(
    model, dummy, "model.onnx",
    opset_version=17,
    input_names=["image"],
    output_names=["logits"],
    dynamic_axes={"image": {0: "batch"}, "logits": {0: "batch"}}
)

# Inference
sess = ort.InferenceSession("model.onnx", providers=["CUDAExecutionProvider", "CPUExecutionProvider"])
output = sess.run(["logits"], {"image": np.random.randn(4, 3, 224, 224).astype(np.float32)})
print(output[0].shape)  # (4, num_classes)

bytes.topic: TensorRT — NVIDIA optimization, quantization, engine building, latency vs accuracy
bytes.title: ONNX + TensorRT — Building Optimized Engines with Quantization and Latency Control
bytes.body: When you export a model to ONNX and target NVIDIA hardware, TensorRT is your fastest path to production-grade latency. The bridge is `tensorrt` (via `trtexec`) or the Python `tensorrt` API — both consume `.onnx` files directly. TensorRT parses your ONNX graph, fuses ops (e.g., Conv+BN+ReLU into a single kernel), and compiles a hardware-specific `.engine` file. INT8 quantization requires a calibration dataset; TensorRT runs inference passes to compute activation ranges per tensor, minimizing accuracy loss. FP16 is lossless for most vision models and cuts latency ~40% on Ampere+. Critical caveat: engines are GPU-architecture-specific — an engine built on an A100 won't run on a T4. At runtime, load the engine with `trt.Runtime`, deserialize it, and bind input/output buffers via `IExecutionContext.execute_async_v2()`. Always profile with `trtexec --onnx=model.onnx --fp16 --best` before committing to a precision mode — latency vs. accuracy tradeoffs are model-dependent and non-obvious.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorrt as trt
import numpy as np

TRT_LOGGER = trt.Logger(trt.Logger.WARNING)

def build_engine(onnx_path: str, fp16: bool = True) -> trt.ICudaEngine:
    with trt.Builder(TRT_LOGGER) as builder, \
         builder.create_network(1 << int(trt.NetworkDefinitionCreationFlag.EXPLICIT_BATCH)) as network, \
         trt.OnnxParser(network, TRT_LOGGER) as parser:
        config = builder.create_builder_config()
        config.set_memory_pool_limit(trt.MemoryPoolType.WORKSPACE, 1 << 30)  # 1GB
        if fp16 and builder.platform_has_fast_fp16:
            config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.FP16)
        with open(onnx_path, "rb") as f:
            parser.parse(f.read())
        return builder.build_serialized_network(network, config)

bytes.topic: Ray — distributed training, Ray Tune for HPO, Ray Serve for model serving
bytes.title: ONNX + Ray — HPO to Production Without a Framework Switch
bytes.body:
Ray Tune runs HPO trials across a cluster, each training a separate model variant in parallel. Once `tune.run()` completes, `result.get_best_checkpoint()` returns the winning trial's checkpoint — export it immediately with `torch.onnx.export(model, dummy_input, "best.onnx", opset_version=17, dynamic_axes={"input": {0: "batch"}})`. Setting `dynamic_axes` on the batch dimension is non-negotiable if Ray Serve will handle variable-size payloads.

Ray Serve's `@serve.deployment` initializes an `ort.InferenceSession` once per replica inside `__init__`, not per request. Set `GraphOptimizationLevel.ORT_ENABLE_ALL` and pass `providers=["CUDAExecutionProvider", "CPUExecutionProvider"]` for automatic GPU fallback. Use `session.get_inputs()[0].name` instead of hardcoding — Tune trials across architectures produce ONNX graphs with different input names.

For horizontal scale, increment `num_replicas`; Ray Serve load-balances across ORT sessions. Enable `SessionOptions.enable_mem_pattern = True` and batch requests inside `__call__` to saturate throughput. Ray's actor isolation means each replica owns its session exclusively, eliminating the GIL contention that makes threading-based ONNX Runtime servers hit a ceiling under concurrent load.

bytes.topic: Hyperparameter Optimization — grid vs random vs Bayesian search, early stopping
bytes.title: ONNX Runtime as Your HPO Evaluation Backend — Grid, Random, and Bayesian Search
bytes.body:
Grid search over ONNX-exported models wastes trials on poor hyperparameter regions — random search samples more efficiently but never adapts. Bayesian search (Optuna's TPE sampler) builds a probabilistic model of the space, directing trials toward regions that improve both accuracy and ORT inference latency simultaneously.

The canonical pattern: train → `torch.onnx.export()` → `ort.InferenceSession` → evaluate. Running evaluation through ORT instead of the native framework ensures your HPO objective reflects production latency, not PyTorch or TensorFlow overhead. A model scoring well in framework-native eval but slow under `GraphOptimizationLevel.ORT_ENABLE_ALL` is not your best candidate.

Early stopping: Optuna's `MedianPruner` kills unpromising trials mid-search. Extend it with an ORT latency gate — if `sess.run()` on a sample batch exceeds your SLA, raise `optuna.TrialPruned()` immediately, before full validation. This dual-objective pruning (accuracy + ORT latency) is what differentiates ORT-backed HPO from naive loops.

For multi-provider targets, run the same `InferenceSession` with `CPUExecutionProvider` and `CUDAExecutionProvider` inside the trial and log both latencies as Optuna user attributes — critical when the same ONNX model deploys across heterogeneous hardware.
bytes.code_snippet:
import optuna
import onnxruntime as ort
import numpy as np

def objective(trial):
    lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-4, 1e-1, log=True)
    hidden = trial.suggest_categorical("hidden", [64, 128, 256])

    model_path = train_and_export_onnx(lr, hidden)  # returns .onnx path
    sess_opts = ort.SessionOptions()
    sess_opts.graph_optimization_level = ort.GraphOptimizationLevel.ORT_ENABLE_ALL
    sess = ort.InferenceSession(model_path, sess_opts, providers=["CPUExecutionProvider"])

    input_name = sess.get_inputs()[0].name
    preds = sess.run(None, {input_name: X_val.astype(np.float32)})[0]
    accuracy = (preds.argmax(1) == y_val).mean()

    latency = np.mean([sess.run(None, {input_name: X_val[:32].astype(np.float32)})
                       for _ in range(10)])  # warm-up benchmark
    if latency > 0.05:
        raise optuna.TrialPruned()

    return accuracy

study = optuna.create_study(
    direction="maximize",
    sampler=optuna.samplers.TPESampler(),
    pruner=optuna.pruners.MedianPruner(n_startup_trials=5, n_warmup_steps=10)
)
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=50)

bytes.topic: Model Serving — REST APIs for inference, batching, latency SLAs
bytes.title: ONNX Runtime — Building Low-Latency REST Inference APIs with Dynamic Batching
bytes.body:
ONNX Runtime's `InferenceSession` is your inference engine — but wrapping it in a REST API without careful design will blow your latency SLAs immediately. The critical path: reuse a single `InferenceSession` across requests (initialization costs 100ms–2s depending on model size and EP), and use `SessionOptions` to pin thread counts via `inter_op_num_threads` and `intra_op_num_threads` to avoid CPU thrashing under load.

For batching, don't wait for a fixed batch size — use a time-bounded queue (e.g., 10ms window) to accumulate requests, then construct a single `numpy` array along axis 0 and run one `session.run()` call. This collapses N separate inference calls into one, leveraging ONNX Runtime's vectorized execution across the batch dimension natively.

Set your SLA budget: P99 < 50ms for small models on CPU EP, P99 < 10ms on CUDA EP with `CUDAExecutionProvider` + `cudnn_conv_algo_search = EXHAUSTIVE`. Profile with `session.run()` timestamps, not framework middleware — middleware overhead is invisible otherwise. Use `IOBinding` to keep tensors on-device between requests when chaining models.
bytes.code_snippet:
import onnxruntime as ort
import numpy as np
from fastapi import FastAPI
import asyncio, time

app = FastAPI()
opts = ort.SessionOptions()
opts.intra_op_num_threads = 4
opts.inter_op_num_threads = 2
session = ort.InferenceSession("model.onnx", opts, providers=["CUDAExecutionProvider", "CPUExecutionProvider"])

batch_queue: list = []
BATCH_WINDOW_MS = 10

@app.post("/infer")
async def infer(data: list[float]):
    future = asyncio.get_event_loop().create_future()
    batch_queue.append((np.array(data, dtype=np.float32), future))
    await asyncio.sleep(BATCH_WINDOW_MS / 1000)
    if not future.done():
        inputs, futures = zip(*batch_queue[:])
        batch_queue.clear()
        batch = np.stack(inputs, axis=0)
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        outputs = session.run(None, {session.get_inputs()[0].name: batch})
        latency_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
        for i, f in enumerate(futures):
            f.set_result({"output": outputs[0][i].tolist(), "latency_ms": latency_ms})
    return await future

bytes.topic: Model Monitoring — prediction drift, data drift, performance degradation in prod
bytes.title: ONNX Runtime — Detecting Model Drift in Production Without Rebuilding Your Pipeline
bytes.body:
ONNX Runtime has no built-in monitoring hooks, so drift detection means wrapping your InferenceSession. The key insight: use raw logits from session.run() as your monitoring signal—not just the argmax—since distribution shape degrades before accuracy visibly drops.

For prediction drift, maintain a rolling window of output tensors and compute L2 distance against a baseline distribution serialized at deploy time (numpy .npy works fine). Threshold on that norm-distance gives early warnings days before accuracy metrics move.

For data drift, intercept inputs before session.run(). Use session.get_inputs() to enumerate named inputs with their expected dtypes and shapes, then log per-dimension mean/std against deployment-time statistics. Population Stability Index (PSI > 0.2 signals significant shift) works well here since it's distribution-agnostic.

For performance degradation, set enable_profiling=True on SessionOptions during periodic sampling runs—never every request. Call session.end_profiling() to get a Chrome-trace JSON. Alert when p99 latency on heavy nodes (MatMul, Gather, LayerNorm) exceeds 2× your deployment baseline. This catches provider-level regressions when CUDA drivers or ExecutionProvider configs change under you.
bytes.code_snippet:
import onnxruntime as ort
import numpy as np
from collections import deque

session = ort.InferenceSession("model.onnx", providers=["CUDAExecutionProvider"])
baseline_mean = np.load("baseline_logits_mean.npy")  # saved at deploy time
window: deque = deque(maxlen=1000)
input_name = session.get_inputs()[0].name

def run_monitored(x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    logits = session.run(None, {input_name: x})[0]
    window.extend(logits.tolist())
    if len(window) == 1000:
        drift = np.linalg.norm(np.mean(window, axis=0) - baseline_mean)
        if drift > 0.15:
            print(f"[PREDICTION DRIFT] L2={drift:.4f}")
    return logits

bytes.topic: CI/CD for ML — automated retraining, evaluation gates, deployment pipelines
bytes.title: ONNX CI/CD Pipelines — Automated Evaluation Gates Before Deployment
bytes.body:
Shipping ML models safely requires more than just exporting to ONNX — it demands structured gates at every stage of the pipeline. Start with a structural gate: run `onnx.checker.check_model()` and `onnx.shape_inference.infer_shapes()` immediately post-export to catch malformed graphs before they hit staging. Pin your opset version explicitly (`opset_imports`) in CI config so runtime upgrades don't silently break operator support.

The critical gate is the evaluation gate: load the `.onnx` artifact with `onnxruntime.InferenceSession`, run it against a frozen validation set, and assert metric thresholds in code — not in a human review step. If `accuracy < 0.94` or `p99_latency_ms > 8`, the pipeline fails and the old model stays live.

For retraining triggers, compare the new model's `onnx.ModelProto.metadata_props` (embed git SHA, dataset hash, eval scores as custom metadata) against the production artifact. Only promote if metrics improve or stay within tolerance. Store artifacts in versioned blob storage and deploy via a blue-green swap that swaps the ORT session pointer atomically — no downtime, instant rollback.
bytes.code_snippet:
import onnx
import onnxruntime as ort
import numpy as np

def evaluation_gate(model_path: str, val_inputs: np.ndarray, val_labels: np.ndarray, threshold: float = 0.94) -> bool:
    onnx.checker.check_model(onnx.load(model_path))

    sess = ort.InferenceSession(model_path, providers=["CPUExecutionProvider"])
    input_name = sess.get_inputs()[0].name
    logits = sess.run(None, {input_name: val_inputs})[0]

    preds = np.argmax(logits, axis=1)
    accuracy = (preds == val_labels).mean()

    print(f"Validation accuracy: {accuracy:.4f} (threshold: {threshold})")
    return float(accuracy) >= threshold

bytes.topic: Feature Stores — centralized feature management, online vs offline features
bytes.title: ONNX Runtime + Feature Stores — Eliminating Training-Serving Skew at Inference
bytes.body:
Training-serving skew is the silent killer of ONNX model performance. Your offline features (Parquet, Delta Lake) go through sklearn pipelines during training — but at inference, your online store (Redis, DynamoDB via Feast/Tecton) must produce byte-identical tensors to what ONNX Runtime expects.

Start by inspecting your model's contract: `session.get_inputs()` returns each input's `name`, `shape`, and `type` (e.g., `tensor(float)`). These names must exactly match what your feature store serves — no silent column renames allowed.

The fix: export your preprocessing pipeline as a separate ONNX graph using `sklearn-onnx` or `tf2onnx`, then chain it with your main model via `onnxruntime` session chaining or merge both into a single graph with `onnx.compose.merge_models`. Now both offline and online paths run the identical ONNX preprocessing graph — skew becomes structurally impossible.

For online latency, pin your `InferenceSession` with `ExecutionMode.ORT_SEQUENTIAL` and pre-allocate `numpy` buffers per feature vector shape. Avoid re-allocating on every request; feature stores return consistent shapes per entity type.
bytes.code_snippet:
import onnxruntime as ort
import numpy as np

session = ort.InferenceSession("chained_model.onnx", providers=["CPUExecutionProvider"])
expected = {i.name: i.shape for i in session.get_inputs()}

# Fetch online features (Feast example)
fv = store.get_online_features(
    features=["user_stats:click_rate", "user_stats:session_count"],
    entity_rows=[{"user_id": uid}]
).to_dict()

feed = {
    name: np.array([[fv[name][0]]], dtype=np.float32)
    for name in expected
}
output = session.run(None, feed)[0]

bytes.topic: Quantization & Pruning — reducing model size, int8/fp16, trade-offs
bytes.title: ONNX Quantization — INT8, FP16, and When Each Actually Wins
bytes.body: ONNX Runtime's quantization tools let you shrink models without retraining. Static INT8 quantization via `quantize_static()` requires a calibration dataset to compute activation ranges — it yields the smallest models (~4× vs FP32) and fastest CPU inference, but accuracy can slip 1–3% on sensitive tasks. Dynamic quantization (`quantize_dynamic()`) skips calibration by computing ranges at runtime; safer for NLP/transformer weights, but slower due to per-operator overhead. FP16 (`convert_float_to_float16()` from `onnxmltools`) is the sweet spot for GPU paths — near-lossless precision with ~2× memory reduction, zero calibration needed. Pruning is separate: ONNX has no native pruning API, so you prune in PyTorch/TensorFlow first, export to ONNX, then quantize. After quantizing, always validate with `onnx.checker.check_model()` and benchmark with `ort.InferenceSession` using `providers=['CPUExecutionProvider']` vs `CUDAExecutionProvider` — provider choice changes which quantized ops get hardware acceleration. Watch for `QuantizeLinear`/`DequantizeLinear` node pairs in Netron; excessive Q/DQ wrapping kills the speedup.
bytes.code_snippet:
import onnxruntime.quantization as ortq

# Static INT8 — needs calibration data
ortq.quantize_static(
    model_input="model.onnx",
    model_output="model_int8.onnx",
    calibration_data_reader=my_calibration_reader,
    quant_format=ortq.QuantFormat.QDQ,
    activation_type=ortq.QuantType.QInt8,
    weight_type=ortq.QuantType.QInt8,
)

# Dynamic INT8 — no calibration, safer for transformers
ortq.quantize_dynamic(
    model_input="model.onnx",
    model_output="model_dynamic_int8.onnx",
    weight_type=ortq.QuantType.QInt8,
)

bytes.topic: A/B Testing Models — shadow deployment, canary releases, champion/challenger
bytes.title: ONNX A/B Testing — Shadow Deployment, Canary Releases, and Champion/Challenger
bytes.body:
Running multiple ONNX models in production simultaneously is straightforward because each InferenceSession is stateless and thread-safe. Shadow deployment means loading both champion and challenger sessions, routing all live traffic to the champion, but asynchronously running the challenger on the same input — comparing outputs without user impact. Use a ConcurrentDictionary<string, InferenceSession> keyed by model variant to manage sessions loaded from versioned .onnx files.

For canary releases, maintain a weighted router: hash the request ID modulo 100 and route to the challenger session if the value falls below your canary percentage (e.g., 5). Both sessions share the same OrtEnvironment singleton — instantiating multiple environments wastes memory and thread pools.

Champion/challenger requires metric collection per session. Tag each InferenceSession with metadata (model version, quantization level, opset) and emit latency and accuracy signals to your observability layer. ONNX Runtime's SessionOptions.AppendExecutionProvider_CUDA lets you pin challenger models to a secondary GPU, isolating resource contention from the champion entirely. Swap champion by atomically replacing the dictionary entry — no restart needed.

bytes.topic: Data Pipelines — feature engineering pipelines, data validation, drift detection
bytes.title: ONNX + skl2onnx — Freeze Feature Pipelines and Catch Data Drift at Serving Time
bytes.body:
Freezing your sklearn preprocessing pipeline as an ONNX graph with skl2onnx eliminates training-serving skew — the same StandardScaler, SimpleImputer, and feature transforms run identically in Python, C#, or C++ via ONNX Runtime. `convert_sklearn()` serializes the full sklearn Pipeline into an ONNX protobuf, storing fitted statistics (mean, variance, fill values) inside the graph's initializer tensors.

For data validation, `InferenceSession.get_inputs()` exposes the expected tensor shape and dtype at runtime. Assert live data against these specs to catch schema drift — feature count mismatches, column reordering, or unexpected type promotions — before they silently corrupt inference outputs.

For statistical drift, export a second ONNX model trained on training-set feature distributions (e.g., a KernelDensity estimator via skl2onnx or a custom graph using `onnx.helper.make_graph`). Run both sessions back-to-back in your serving loop: the feature pipeline transforms inputs, the drift detector scores them. Because both are native ONNX Runtime sessions, there's no Python overhead between steps. On CPU, drift scoring a 512-row, 10-feature batch typically adds under 1ms — cheap enough to run on every request.
bytes.code_snippet:
from skl2onnx import convert_sklearn
from skl2onnx.common.data_types import FloatTensorType
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
import onnxruntime as rt
import numpy as np

pipe = Pipeline([("imputer", SimpleImputer()), ("scaler", StandardScaler())])
pipe.fit(X_train)

onnx_model = convert_sklearn(pipe, initial_types=[("X", FloatTensorType([None, X_train.shape[1]]))])
with open("feat_pipe.onnx", "wb") as f:
    f.write(onnx_model.SerializeToString())

sess = rt.InferenceSession("feat_pipe.onnx")
meta = sess.get_inputs()[0]
assert X_live.shape[1] == meta.shape[1], f"Schema drift: expected {meta.shape[1]} features, got {X_live.shape[1]}"
out = sess.run(None, {"X": X_live.astype(np.float32)})[0]

bytes.topic: Infrastructure — GPU cluster management, spot instances for training, cost optimization
bytes.title: ONNX Runtime on Spot Clusters — Checkpoint Resilience and CUDA Arena Tuning
bytes.body:
Spot instance preemptions are the biggest threat to long ONNX Runtime training runs. ORT's `onnxruntime.training.api.CheckpointState` combined with `TrainingSession.save_checkpoint()` gives you a portable, fast-serializing recovery point that reloads in seconds — critical when a preempted node rejoins a cluster mid-epoch.

On the GPU memory side, ORT's CUDA execution provider exposes `arena_extend_strategy` and `gpu_mem_limit` in `CUDAExecutionProviderOptions`. Setting `arena_extend_strategy=1` (kSameAsRequested) avoids ORT's default aggressive pre-allocation, which can cause OOM on shared spot nodes. Pair this with `do_copy_in_default_stream=False` to overlap H2D transfers with compute on multi-stream setups.

For multi-GPU spot clusters, pin `OrtValueInfo` bindings via `IOBinding` rather than re-allocating device buffers each checkpoint restore — this cuts cold-start latency by 30–60% on A100 nodes. Use `SessionOptions.add_session_config_entry('session.use_device_allocator_for_initializers', '1')` to keep frozen weights in the CUDA arena across checkpoint cycles, avoiding repeated PCIe transfers on node recovery.
bytes.code_snippet:
import onnxruntime.training.api as orttraining
import onnxruntime as ort

cuda_opts = ort.CUDAExecutionProviderOptions()
cuda_opts.arena_extend_strategy = 1          # kSameAsRequested — no over-alloc
cuda_opts.gpu_mem_limit = 20 * 1024**3       # 20 GB cap for shared spot node
cuda_opts.do_copy_in_default_stream = False  # overlap H2D with compute

checkpoint_state = orttraining.CheckpointState.load_checkpoint("ckpt.ortckpt")
model = orttraining.Module("model.onnx", checkpoint_state, "loss.onnx",
                           device="cuda", providers=[("CUDAExecutionProvider", cuda_opts)])

# Save on SIGTERM (spot preemption signal)
import signal
signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, lambda s, f: orttraining.CheckpointState.save_checkpoint(checkpoint_state, "ckpt.ortckpt"))
